content.text: As Alan Lerner memorably wrote in My Fair Lady, ‘ding dong, I’m getting married in the morning’.

Between final dress fittings, guest lists, playlist debates and a growing pile of “just in case” items, wedding planning has been keeping me busy. There are a lot of decisions involved in bringing a wedding together, from what you wear to how the day is captured and remembered.
It turns out that many of those decisions are shaped by creativity, ideas and innovation, often protected through intellectual property (IP).
Getting dressed: where design meets IP

For me the most important part of my big day (apart from my future husband) is the dress. Since I was a child, I have dreamed of the dress I will wear for my wedding, and now that dream is becoming a reality.
Whether you choose to wear a beautiful dress, a bespoke suit, or something borrowed and beautifully worn-in, what you’re wearing reflects hours of creative work.

If you’re creating something yourself, it’s worth understanding how intellectual property fits in. In a previous post on The Great British Sewing Bee: copyright and a homemade wedding dress, we explored how you can copy or adapt patterns for personal use while still respecting the original designer’s rights.

Designers often protect the look of their creations using registered designs, which may include the shape, configuration, pattern or decoration of items like clothing and accessories. Well-known brands also use trade marks to protect their names and logos, helping you know you’re buying the real thing rather than an imitation.

Even the practical elements of getting ready can involve innovation. From adjustable garment bags to clever fastening systems, some products rely on patented inventions designed to make life a little easier.

Take the train of a wedding dress. It looks beautiful, but it is not always practical once the dancing starts. Patent GB2566676, granted to Trainloop Limited, covers a detachable fastener designed to lift and secure the train so it does not trail on the ground. It’s a small detail, but one that makes a big difference on a busy day.
The soundtrack to the day

Music sets the tone for everything from walking down the aisle to the last song of the evening. Each track played is the result of songwriting, performance and recording, so copyright should be considered when making your choices.

Those walking down the aisle to modern favourites such as Ed Sheeran will need to ensure their venue has the appropriate licence. More traditional choices, such as Mendelssohn’s Wedding March or Handel’s Arrival of the Queen of Sheba, are copyright free if being played live, but may need a licence for a modern recording.

At weddings, venues and DJs usually handle music licensing behind the scenes, making sure the rights of composers and performers are respected. In the UK, this is typically covered through licences from PRS for Music (which looks after songwriters and composers) and PPL (which represents performers and record producers), often combined into a single licence.

Even that carefully curated playlist you spend weeks perfecting plays a part. While the song list itself isn’t protected, the individual tracks may well be, reminding us how deeply IP is woven into everyday experiences.
Capturing the memories

Photographers and videographers don’t just document a day. Through their choices around framing, lighting and editing, they create original works. The images and films they produce are protected by copyright, and usually belong to the person who created them, unless agreed otherwise.

When choosing our photographer, I made sure to read the contract carefully so I fully understood how we could use the images afterwards, while still respecting the photographer’s copyright. It covered things like sharing photos on social media and how the images could be used more widely. Having that clarity upfront meant there were no surprises later.

The technology behind the camera matters too. Many modern cameras, drones and editing tools rely on patented innovations developed to improve image quality, stabilisation and storage, all helping ensure those memories last. We’ve explored this in more detail in our blog A snapshot of innovation: the history of photography through the lens of IP, which looks at how IP has shaped photography from its earliest days to the technology we use now.
Why IP matters beyond the big day

Weddings are personal, emotional and often months in the making. Planning mine has meant making lots of small decisions, each one supported by someone’s creative work or ideas. From what we wear, to the music we choose, to the photos we’ll look back on for years to come, IP is part of what helps those ideas come together.

So, while I might not be getting married “in the morning” just yet, I know that when the day arrives, it will be shaped by the creativity and innovation behind so many of the things that make it special.
